issue_title: Server disconnects regularly
issue_body:
My Websocket server is the application ProPresenter 6 (free trial download available if that helps). I'm trying to grab data from the `/stagedisplay` endpoint (127.0.0.1 so LAN/WAN involved), and it's working really well using lomond. I'm getting very close to being able to receive the data I need from ProPresenter to set up some other automation. Thank you!

However, about every 8 seconds or so (give or take `back_off` rate), the server side (PP) sends a TCP RST and lomond has to reconnect. As far as I can tell from looking at the traffic with Wireshark, the ping/pong behavior is correct but that was my first suspicion. Further, I'm able to connect, authenticate, send requests, get responses etc. The disconnections have me a bit baffled though.

Before finding lomond, I grabbed _WebSocket Client_[1] from the Apple App Store. While it's got some frustrating UI issues, it does not seem to suffer from the same disconnects. More specifically, _WebSocket Client_ seems to be doing something that is preventing PP from wanting to send a TCP RST, as per Wireshark. I'm not sure what that magic is.

I've attached a sample Wireshark pcap file (`62576` is the server side port), as well as linked a simple example gist[2] that uses lomond. It seems like there's a bug somewhere in this pile of fun, but I'm not sure where it could be.

[pro6-lomond.pcapng.zip](https://github.com/wildfoundry/dataplicity-lomond/files/2330464/pro6-lomond.pcapng.zip)

[1] https://itunes.apple.com/us/app/websocket-client/id1399498094?mt=12
[2] https://gist.github.com/rjhornsby/ed74d386672abe60896ca93d6534e7a1

issue_title: Select vs *Poll
issue_body:
select cannot select on file descriptor numbers > FD_SETSIZE (1024 on
most systems), limiting the number of connections that can be created.

It does not necessarily have to be websocket connections, but if the application already has a high number of file descriptor usage, then the websocket connection would fail...

So, why not use any available poll implementation?

I found out about lomond while searching for a python websocket python implementation. One of the search results was your blog post and the reasoning why lomond was created. You mentioned websocket-client. They seemed to have replaced select with poll, https://github.com/websocket-client/websocket-client/pull/375

Anyway, any plans to support poll instead of select?

issue_title: Sam file header not written when using non-binary output
issue_body:
Hi there,
Maybe I'm missing something, but when I do the following:
```
samfile = pysam.AlignmentFile("-", "r")
samout = pysam.AlignmentFile("output.sam", "w", template=samfile)
testread = samfile.next()
samout.write(testread)
samout.close()

```
The read is in the output sam file but the header isn't. I'm actually not interested in writing sam files per se but outputting to stdout without any compression slowing me down. I tried versions `0.9.0` and `0.9.1.4`.

Any advice how to force output the header?

issue_body:
I have a BAM file generated from aligning paired-end reads to hg19, which was then indexed with samtools index to create a .bai file. I want to count the number of reads overlapping each base in a given region using pileup. But pysam is giving me incorrect coordinates, for example (simple fetch example):

`import pysam`
`bam = pysam.AlignmentFile('file.bam')`
`reads = bam.fetch('chr3',50617849,50618103)`
`for read in reads:`
    `print read.pos`

the first coordinate printed is 50335428 (not even within the region), instead of the expected 50617849. Why is this behavior exhibited? I can provide more details if needed.

issue_title: 'az resource update' gets confused if property value contains an equal sign
issue_body:
e.g. consider the following command line, which works fine:

    az resource update --resource-group MyRG --namespace Microsoft.Web --parent sites/MySite --resource-type Extensions --name MSDeploy --set properties.packageUri=https://foo.com/bar.zip --api-version 2015-08-01

But now suppose the url I want to use for the packageUri has a query string, e.g.

    az resource update --resource-group MyRG --namespace Microsoft.Web --parent sites/MySite --resource-type Extensions --name MSDeploy --set properties.packageUri=https://foo.com/bar.zip?a=5 --api-version 2015-08-01

This fails with:

> Couldn't find 'packageUri=https://foo' in 'properties.packageUri=https://foo'. Available options: ['complete', 'deployer', 'endtime', 'provisioningstate', 'starttime']

The = sign is confusing the parsing logic. I tried quoting in various way, but did not find one that worked. e.g same error with `--set properties.packageUri="https://foo.com/bar.zip?a=5"`

issue_title: Implement FDR estimation and control for HMM predictions
issue_body:
For some applications HMM states (or their combinations) represent distinct hypotheses (in the statistical sense). In such a setting we might be interested in:

* estimating FDR for the predictions produced by the model,
* constructing a vector of predictions satisfying an upper bound on the FDR.

See [this work](http://www-stat.wharton.upenn.edu/~tcai/paper/FDR-HMM.pdf) by W. Sun and T. Cai for details.

issue_title: Package aiohttp-2.0.6-1.tar.gz make PyPI reports a inexistent 2.0.6.post1 release for pip
issue_body:
## Long story short

There is a `aiohttp-2.0.6-1.tar.gz` at aiohttp's PyPI that `breaks pip.index.PackageFinder` that returns an inexistent `2.0.6.post1` release.

When I ran a tool to update project requirements (like `pip-tools` or an IDE like PyCharm) they report an release newer than `2.0.6` (`2.0.6.post1`) that cannot be installed correctly.

```
DEBUG:pip.index:Found link https://pypi.python.org/packages/66/3f/eac06f68743e2654915650de34893391ff1006cd144a48655d2f75084e17/aiohttp-2.0.6-1.tar.gz#md5=1720
```
## Steps to reproduce

```python
>>> from requests import Session
>>> from pip.index import PackageFinder
>>> f = PackageFinder(index_urls=['https://pypi.python.org/simple'], find_links=[], session=Session())
>>> c = f.find_all_candidates("aiohttp")
>>> [str(x.version) for x in c if "2.0.6" in str(x)]
['2.0.6', '2.0.6.post1']
```

Trying to install it:

```shell
Collecting aiohttp==2.0.6.post1
  Downloading aiohttp-2.0.6-1.tar.gz (1.1MB)
  Requested aiohttp==2.0.6.post1 from https://pypi.python.org/packages/66/3f/eac06f68743e2654915650de34893391ff1006cd144a48655d2f75084e17/aiohttp-2.0.6-1.tar.gz#md5=17207ea5e9fe0a4e16d2cc02f683efe2, but installing version 2.0.6
```

## Fix proposal

Remove `https://pypi.python.org/packages/66/3f/eac06f68743e2654915650de34893391ff1006cd144a48655d2f75084e17/aiohttp-2.0.6-1.tar.gz` from PyPI.

issue_title: API code directory + docs
issue_body:
I wanted to discuss where the API code is going. 

Right now, Jude has the interface defined in [blockstack_client/rpc.py](https://github.com/blockstack/blockstack-core/blob/rc-0.14.1b/blockstack_client/rpc.py). The old API code is in the [api](https://github.com/blockstack/blockstack-core/tree/master/api) directory.

I don't think this is important for the current sprint but we should have the new API code in the api/ directory as I feel that's the most natural place new developers will look for it. 

We've seen issues earlier about people [looking for the interfaces and docs](https://github.com/blockstack/blockstack-core/issues/260).

Our rule of thumb is to put all docs & protocol specs in blockstack/blockstack repo but for the API I think it makes sense to list the API interfaces here. Just like we have the installation instructions here, the API docs are part of "how to use this software". 

Let me know what you guys think. I started a [related issue on the blockstack-desktop-specs](https://github.com/blockstack/blockstack-desktop-specs/issues/8) repo (for moving API docs).

Two decision items are: 

 - [ ] where should the code live (I'm proposing the api/ folder)
 - [ ] where should the docs live (I'm proposing the api/ folder)

We can close this issue when we have a decision on this.

CC: @jcnelson, @larrysalibra, @shea256

When we load test an internal API that calls Clarifai with a known model name, we sometimes get an `UnboundLocalError`:

at get (/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/clarifai/rest/client.py:1340)

issue_title: How to debug a reactive charm
issue_body:
Stolen from [Ask Ubuntu](https://askubuntu.com/questions/804465/juju-charms-how-to-run-and-debug-reactive-charm):

> I'm developing a juju charm in python using a reactive pattern. All my hooks are called using decorators provided by 'basic' base layer. When trying to deploy the charm I encountered an error. In order to solve the error I used 'juju debug-hooks' and 'juju dhx -r' (dhx debugging plugin) but I can't find a way to run charm code using python following reactive pattern... As I understand, (when using dhx for debugging for example) the charm execution is returned to the state exactly before the hook that caused an error and the developer is logged in into a remote debugging session. It is on the developer to run hooks. Normally these hooks would be under /hooks directory and would be run by just executing them. This isn't possible using reactive pattern, since there is no entry point for hooks but everything is just a cascade of reactions to states...

I think this question raises two good points:
1. We should maintain a section for frequently asked questions that can occasionally be updated based on feedback on Ask Ubuntu, #juju, or the mailing lists.
2. In a case of this question, we should link to the relevant Juju documentation about debugging a charm in general, and then talk about the specific case of debugging a reactive charm.

issue_title: Make load scheduler configurable
issue_body:
I have several projects where the distribution of tests runtime is quite scattered, eg:

- 1000 tests of 10ms
- 100 tests of 1 minute

The current load scheduler comes short in this case, as it often ends up sending a batch of slow tests to the same worker.

As a workaround, I use a forked LoadScheduler that uses a fixed queue size (which I use with the minimum value of 2 -> each worker only has one test in its queue at any time):
```
class FixedLocalQueueLoadScheduling(LoadScheduling):  # no cover
    """
    A fork of pytest-xdist default load scheduler that uses a fixed size for workers local queue size.
    """

    def __init__(self, config, log=None, queue_size=2):
        super().__init__(config, log)
        if queue_size < 2:
            raise ValueError('Queue size must be at least 2')
        self.queue_size = queue_size

    def check_schedule(self, node, duration=0):
        if node.shutting_down:
            return

        if self.pending:
            node_pending = self.node2pending[node]
            if len(node_pending) < self.queue_size:
                num_send = self.queue_size - len(node_pending)
                self._send_tests(node, num_send)
        self.log("num items waiting for node:", len(self.pending))

    def schedule(self):
        assert self.collection_is_completed

        # Initial distribution already happened, reschedule on all nodes
        if self.collection is not None:
            for node in self.nodes:
                self.check_schedule(node)
            return

        # allow nodes to have different collections
        if not self._check_nodes_have_same_collection():
            self.log('**Different tests collected, aborting run**')
            return

        # Collections are identical, create the index of pending items.
        self.collection = list(self.node2collection.values())[0]
        self.pending[:] = range(len(self.collection))
        if not self.collection:
            return

        # Send a batch of tests to run. If we don't have at least two
        # tests per node, we have to send them all so that we can send
        # shutdown signals and get all nodes working.
        initial_batch = min(len(self.pending), self.queue_size * len(self.nodes))

        # distribute tests round-robin up to the batch size
        # (or until we run out)
        nodes = cycle(self.nodes)
        for i in range(initial_batch):
            self._send_tests(next(nodes), 1)

        if not self.pending:
            # initial distribution sent all tests, start node shutdown
            for node in self.nodes:
                node.shutdown()
```

It would be nice to have at least one of these propositions implemented in xdist:

1.  Integrate this scheduler (or an even simpler version where queue_size=2)
2.  Make LoadScheduler configurable, so that users can provide initial_batch_size / items_per_node_min / items_per_node_max
3.  When sending a batch of jobs to a node, shuffle like for the initial batch
4. Maybe improve/reduce a bit the defaults settings for initial_batch_size / items_per_node_min / items_per_node_max

issue_title: Extensibility of type assertions
issue_body:

issue_title: Extremely simple http-01 in apache
issue_body:
How about implementing `http-01` for Apache? After writing #1268 I realized that the authenticator would not even have to touch users config - just drop a file in `/etc/apache2/conf-enabled/` with the following contents:

```
<IfModule mod_headers.c>
    <LocationMatch "/.well-known/acme-challenge/*">
        Header set Content-Type "application/jose+json"
        DocumentRoot /var/lib/letsencrypt/apache-http01-root
    </LocationMatch>
</IfModule>
```

reload the daemon, drop necessary resources in `/var/lib/letsencrypt/apache-http01-root`, and voila!

The biggest advantage is that we might even get away without read access (parsing) of the existing configs, not even mentioning write access :) That means less scope for errors (a lot of users don't even get to the authorization stage, because of MisconfigurationErrors raised in the parsing step).

Possible problems:
- users don't have `/etc/apache/conf-enabled` by default
- `DocumentRoot` seems to be allowed there (https://httpd.apache.org/docs/2.2/mod/core.html#documentroot), but if it's not, then we can drop the stanza and try to read the config file and find existing `public_html`

@jdkasten?

issue_title: domain string in user config limited to 64 chars
issue_body:
I've initially posted it at the wrong place ([see here](https://github.com/letsencrypt/acme-spec/issues/232)), from where I was sent to [another wrong place](https://github.com/letsencrypt/boulder/issues/1117) – so I hope try number 3 hits the right place now:

The client accepts a config file using the `-c <filename>` parameter. I'm using this to setup things. With new domains whitelisted, I had to create a new certificate – so I've setup my config file, putting the host names in the `domain = name, altname1, altname2` line. On call, the [client crashed](http://pastebin.com/vs60WXn2):

    Error: [('asn1 encoding routines', 'ASN1_mbstring_ncopy', 'string too long')]

Bad thing, as wildcards are not permitted and the cert (in this case) *must* hold all the names. Figured I can work around this via the `-d` command-line parameter (`-d name -d altname1 -d altname2`), and the cert was created fine. Checked the `.conf` file in `/etc/letsencrypt/renewal` – and found the `domain =` line being exactly as quoted above.

**Verdict:** this limitation just applies to the `-c <config>` file, but not to the renewal config file. Work-around exists as described – but it still would be nice to see this fixed.

issue_title: Pull quote embed
Allow widgets to be embedded in articles
issue_body:
We want to add a new article embed type that allows the user to embed a widget in an article (just like an image, video, etc).

Embeds are currently defined here: https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/tree/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/vendor/dispatch-editor/embeds

and are loaded by the editor here: https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/components/ArticleEditor/ArticleContentEditor.js#L14-L20

I'm proposing we create a new `WidgetEmbed` that allows the user to select a widget and fill out the fields, much like how the widget editor currently works. 

You should be able to use [WidgetSelectInput](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/components/inputs/WidgetSelectInput.js) to give uses the ability to choose the widget. Once a widget is chosen, the fields should be rendered below the input, much like the [current zone editor](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/components/ZoneEditor/index.js#L38-L77).

On the backend, we should define a default zone called "Embed" in `dispatch/theme` to represent an article embed. This allows users to define which of their widgets are compatible with article embeds.

To save widget embed data, you'll need to define a new embed controller to handle widgets. I suggest using [CodeController](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/components/ZoneEditor/index.js#L38-L77) as an example. The `render` method should return the result of rendering the widget with the data provided in the editor. I believe you can accomplish this by saving the widget ID and data as JSON.

As always, feel free to reach out on Slack if you have any questions!

issue_title: Array input with integers results in "value type not convertible"
issue_body:
Repro steps:

```python
spec = coremltools.proto.Model_pb2.Model()
spec.specificationVersion = 1
spec.identity.MergeFromString('')
input = spec.description.input.add()
input.type.multiArrayType.shape.append(3)
input.type.multiArrayType.dataType = coremltools.proto.FeatureTypes_pb2.ArrayFeatureType.INT32
input.name = "input"
output = spec.description.output.add()
output.type.multiArrayType.shape.append(3)
output.type.multiArrayType.dataType = coremltools.proto.FeatureTypes_pb2.ArrayFeatureType.INT32
output.name = "output"
model = coremltools.models.MLModel(spec)
model.predict({'input': [1,2,3]})
```

Expected: something like
```python
{u'input': array([ 1.,  2.,  3.])}
```

Actual:
```python
/Users/zach/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/coremltools/models/model.pyc in predict(self, data, useCPUOnly, **kwargs)
    318 
    319         if self.__proxy__:
--> 320             return self.__proxy__.predict(data,useCPUOnly)
    321         else:
    322             if _macos_version() < (10, 13):

RuntimeError: value type not convertible
```

Note that changing the input to `[1.0, 2, 3]` seems to fix the issue; so despite the multiArrayType being INT32, it only seems to allow float input (at least in some cases).

issue_title: `awsume -r` should reload credentials that were previous acquired with `awsume -a` flag
issue_body:
`awsume -r <role>` reloads <role> credentials, but `awsume -r` expects a profile name. 

I think `awsume -r` without a role name should reload _ALL_ profiles that were previously executed with `awsume -a <role_name>`. This way I don't have to remember which profiles I had previously configured to automatically refresh.

Moreover, I think `awsume -r <rolename>` should act l like `awsume -a <rolename>; awsume -r <rolename>` if `awsume -a` was **not** run previously.

Great program! I love it and use it daily for my consulting work. Keep up the good work.

issue_title: Question: how do I retrieved a saved artifact object from MongoObserver?
issue_body:
I'm able to set up a MongoObserver  and use `add_artifact` to save the _file_ where I store (say) a pickled object `MyTestDataTensor`.  My question then is, how do I go back to say "run number 3" and retrieve `MyTestDataTensor` directly from the mongo observer? When I do this:

```
db = ex.observers[0]
db.run_entry['artifacts']
```
I get the _objectIDs_ associated with my saved objects, but what do I do with those? I'd like a programmatic way to retrieve my saved object. I know I could get the saved file name and "manually" load it into my code, but I'm hoping there's a way to directly retrieve it from the MongoObserver via a mongodb-type query.

Maybe I'm missing something, but I've looked around quite a bit and can't find it.

Thanks

issue_title: include docs for new top parser
issue_body:
@jandom, please add appropriate links and files to the sphinx docs so that the doc strings for the new top parser PR #25 also show up in the [online docs under core.formats](http://gromacswrapper.readthedocs.org/en/develop/gromacs/core/formats.html). In particular, you need an entry in [doc/sphinx/source/gromacs/core/formats.txt](https://github.com/Becksteinlab/GromacsWrapper/blob/develop/doc/sphinx/source/gromacs/core/formats.txt) to a file `core/fileformats/top_parser.txt` with content like the following
```rest
Gromacs Preprocessed Topology (top) Parser
===================================

.. versionadded:: 0.4.1

Gromacs can produce *preprocessed topology files* that contain *all* topology information (``grompp -pp processed.top``). The :class:`~gromacs.fileformats.top.TOP` parser can read an write these files.

.. toctree::
   :maxdepth: 1
   
    top.txt
    blocks.txt
```

Also update the `CHANGES` file.

issue_title: Clearing the queue triggers a Application error
issue_body:
When we clear the queue we call `resetSong()` to clear the current song info by [manually populating a fake Mopidy Tltrack object](https://github.com/pimusicbox/mopidy-musicbox-webclient/blob/64eda54a78ebe90c31fdc8003a86533ae0108632/mopidy_musicbox_webclient/static/js/gui.js#L12-L19) and passing it to `setSongInfo()`. We then end up trying to get images for the uri `' '` (a space character) which Mopidy doesn't like. Mopidy log available [here](http://pastebin.com/rG0LmB2n).

```
{"message": "Application error", "code": 0, "data": {"message": "Expected a list of URIs, not u\' \'",
```
This is kind of related to https://github.com/pimusicbox/mopidy-musicbox-webclient/issues/172

on the file (zipped due to github requirements)

(:name "Z3")

sat
(($Out_Produce$4025$15$2$ "IR@73@19@0@"))
(($Out_ProduceReport$$$3973$5264$15$2$ (ProduceReport$C_ProduceReport
  (ItemInfo$C_ItemInfo (ItemId$C_ItemId 73) (ItemSize$C_ItemSize 19))

i.e. include the time value of 4 as well!

Could you please look into this issue?
Thanks in advance,

Pierre

issue_title: Z3 opt fails when minimizing BV on unsat instances
issue_body:
The following example
```
from z3 import *

x = BitVec("x", 64)

zero = BitVecVal(0, 64)
one = BitVecVal(1, 64)

s = Optimize()
s.add(one < zero, x > 0)

# h = s.maximize(-x)
h = s.minimize(x)

res = s.check()

print(h.lower())
print(h.upper())
print(res)
print(s.model())
```
fails with
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 14, in <module>
    res = s.check()
  File "src/api/python/z3.py", line 6519, in check
    return CheckSatResult(Z3_optimize_check(self.ctx.ref(), self.optimize))
  File "src/api/python/z3core.py", line 3523, in Z3_optimize_check

If `s.minimize(x)` is changed to `s.maximize(-x)`, everything works as expected.

issue_title: pose_from_oxts_packet method
issue_body:
Hello, thanks for your implementation !

I have been looking at your code to get a pose vector from gps coordinates here : https://github.com/utiasSTARS/pykitti/blob/master/pykitti/utils.py#L63

At first the mercator projection made sense, but I wonder how off it can become compared to real XYZ data, i.e Earth Centered, Earth Fixed (ECEF) x-y-z values

I'd say that if you are not outrageously close to a pole it should not be a problem, especially because RTK data is at best 1cm precise.

So converting GPS to ECEF and then back to a rotated centered system is clearly overkill.

What I don't understand then is why having some complicated function as the one used in mercator projection ? if we compute the derivative of function used for `ty` , we get ... `1/cos(lat)` which evens out the scale used in the first place. So the taylor expansion is the same as when we simply use `ty = er * (lat - lat_0)` (`lat_0` is the latitude of first frame)

In addition, just using `ty = er * (lat - lat_0)` might seem more closely related to actual position because the error induced is the same when we got north or south, which is not the case for mercator which will expand lengths when going north and compress them for south.

The main point still remains that in German roads for KITTI we don't have north pole problems so why not use a simpler function for `ty`?

issue_title: `nwbfile.create_ogen_site` device argument expects string
issue_body: the `device` argument of `nwbfile.create_ogen_site` takes a string that is the name of the device. I think it should also take the device object. This will make usage more consistent with `nwbfile.create_electrode_group` and `nwbfile.add_electrode` (with `electrode_group`) where the object is given, not the name of the object. Is there a reason the name of the device is given here?

issue_title: Allow providing effective wavelength, thus bypassing the RSR data
issue_body:
#### Code Sample, a minimal, complete, and verifiable piece of code

```python
# Your code here

```
#### Problem description

The atmospheric correction in the visible range currently requires access to the satellite sensor RSR functions. Many sensors are already available in PySPectral. However, it would be nice/helpful if one could derive the Rayleigh correction without having the RSR implemented in PySpectral. It would just require the user to provide a central or rayleigh scattering effective wavelength, supposing the user could calculate this herself.

#### Code example
Potentially the code could look like this:

```
from pyspectral.rayleigh import Rayleigh
viirs = Rayleigh('Suomi-NPP', 'viirs')
corr = viirs.get_reflectance(sun_zenith, sat_zenith, azidiff, effective_wavelength=0.486, refl_m05)
```
Currently the last call as to llok like this:
```
corr = viirs.get_reflectance(sun_zenith, sat_zenith, azidiff, 'M03', refl_m05)
```

Thank you for reporting an issue !

issue_title: Auto-retry for failing DIRAC requests in 6.3.0
issue_body:
Hi all,

I've committed large changes to catch DIRAC exceptions at the lowest level in Ganga and raise an appropriate error.

My question is should we attempt to retry all/most requests which fail automatically now?
I don't see much harm in retrying each command say 5 times and have some function option to request this be deactivated for some certain calls.
(Obviously we can avoid this for failures due to invalid proxies etc).

What are people's opinions on this?

Rob

issue_title: Ordering isn't consistent for NULL values
issue_body:
If you post some data with only the key `foo`, and then later add the key `bar`, all the previous records will have NULL for the `bar` key, which makes sense.
However, if you then sort on the `bar` key, you'll have some records with `NULL` in the middle of the list, instead of at the beginning or the end (which would arguably make more sense).

issue_title: citing PyWavelets
issue_body:
A user on the mailing list recently [asked how best to cite PyWavelets](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/pywavelets/OE-OeefLsrk).

 @PyWavelets/core: 
There is not currently a PyWavelets-specific journal article, but it would still be good to have a way for users to cite the software.  What do you think about making it citeable via something like [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/)?  A nice feature there seems to be that you can have a top-level DOI that always references the current release or the user can cite a specific release.

Are there other alternatives that would be worth considering as well?

issue_title: Participantstats item list position not respected
issue_body:
```
@lazy_property
    @load_match_on_attributeerror
    def items(self) -> List[Item]:
        ids = [self._data[ParticipantStatsData].item0,
               self._data[ParticipantStatsData].item1,
               self._data[ParticipantStatsData].item2,
               self._data[ParticipantStatsData].item3,
               self._data[ParticipantStatsData].item4,
               self._data[ParticipantStatsData].item5,
               self._data[ParticipantStatsData].item6
        ]
        version = _choose_staticdata_version(self.__match)
        return SearchableList([Item(id=id, version=version, region=self.__match.region) for id in ids if id])
```
Currently the list will only contain items that exist. It would be useful to have the position of each item preserved as well (eg. to see a relationship between active items and key binds). 

I can think of 2 ways of solving this:
1. Add properties for item0-6 to the stats object
2. Add empty items to the list to let it contain everything (or at least an option to with a param).

issue_title: 30 second Failure Detection and Process Failover
issue_body: This is to test a rather large story: https://pulp.plan.io/issues/2509 ## Sanity Checking Start Pulp normally and perform a basic sync to sanity check Pulp. start Pulp normally. Allow the system to idle for 40 seconds and observe the logs. Verify that no errors are reported while Pulp idles over 40 seconds. ## Upgrade test This is a 1-time manual test on rpm based installation on EL7. This is not a dev test and likely should not be automated. Upgrade to this release from an earlier release of Pulp. Verify that the /usr/lib/systemd/system/pulp_resource_manager.service contains the line: --heartbeat-interval=5 ## Worker Failure Testing With Pulp normally started, look at the output of `pulp-admin status` and verify all expected workers are present. Then kill -9 a specific worker, for example reserved_resource_worker-0 with sudo pkill -9 -f reserved_resource_worker-0. After 30 seconds have passed check `pulp-admin status` Verify that the worker is no longer shown. It is expected that the status API will not show a killed worker 30 seconds after the kill occurs. Verify that errors are shown in the log that the worker has gone missing. ## Resource Manager Failover Testing ### Testing normal concurrent operation Start Pulp normally, but keep the resource manager stopped. Then in one terminal on the box run one resource manager A with: sudo -u apache /bin/python /usr/bin/celery worker -A pulp.server.async.app -n resource_manager@boxA -Q resource_manager -c 1 --events --umask 18 --pidfile=/var/run/pulp/resource_managerA.pid --heartbeat-interval=5 On a second terminal run resource manager B with: sudo -u apache /bin/python /usr/bin/celery worker -A pulp.server.async.app -n resource_manager@boxB -Q resource_manager -c 1 --events --umask 18 --pidfile=/var/run/pulp/resource_managerB.pid --heartbeat-interval=5 Verify that you see the full Celery "banner" with stars in resource manager A. Verify that you see a log statement that resource manager A has acquired the lock. Verify that you do not see any output from resource manager B. Verify that you see a log statement that resource manager B is a hot spare. Verify that both resource_managers are reported via pulp-admin status ### Failover due to graceful shutdown Start both resource_manager started processes as described above. Ctrl+C from resource manager A Verify that within 5 seconds the logs emit a statement like new lock acquired by 'resource_manager@yyyyyyy' Verify that you see a log statement stating that failover has occurred, and resource manager B is the new primary resource manager Verify that resource manager B displays the celery banner with stars within 5 seconds of the Ctrl-C Verify that only resource manager B is shown in pulp-admin status ### Failover due to killing Start both resource_manager started processes as described above. kill -9 the resource_manager that has acquired the lock Verify that resource manager B displays the celery banner with stars within 30 seconds of the kill Verify that within 5 seconds the logs emit a statement like new lock acquired by 'resource_manager@yyyyyyy' Verify that you see a log statement stating that failover has occurred, and resource manager B is the new primary resource manager Verify that only resource manager B is shown in pulp-admin status ## Celerybeat Failover Testing This can't be done on 1 computer without changing Pulp code due to both celerybeats having the same name. You can do the same test on two machines with one celerybeat on one, and a second on another. I'm going to apply this diff to the code to allow me to do it on 1 machine in two separate terms. ```diff diff --git a/server/pulp/server/async/scheduler.py b/server/pulp/server/async/scheduler.py index d745406..b158386 100644 --- a/server/pulp/server/async/scheduler.py +++ b/server/pulp/server/async/scheduler.py @@ -28,8 +28,9 @@ import pulp.server.logs # noqa _logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) +import random # setting the celerybeat name -CELERYBEAT_NAME = constants.SCHEDULER_WORKER_NAME + "@" + platform.node() +CELERYBEAT_NAME = constants.SCHEDULER_WORKER_NAME + "@" + str(random.randint(1,10000)) class EventMonitor(threading.Thread): ``` ### Testing normal concurrency operation Start Pulp normally, but keep celerybeat stopped. Then in one terminal on the box run celerybeat A with: sudo -u apache /bin/python /usr/bin/celery beat --app=pulp.server.async.celery_instance.celery --scheduler=pulp.server.async.scheduler.Scheduler --pidfile=/var/run/pulp/celerybeatA.pid On a second terminal run celerybeat B with: sudo -u apache /bin/python /usr/bin/celery beat --app=pulp.server.async.celery_instance.celery --scheduler=pulp.server.async.scheduler.Scheduler --pidfile=/var/run/pulp/celerybeatB.pid Verify that pulp-admin status shows both scheduler@ entries Verify that the logs contain a statement like 'New lock acquired by scheduler@xxxxxxxx' Verify that a log statement exists identifying the extra celerybeat as a hot spare. ### Failover due to graceful shutdown Start both celerybeat processes as described above. Ctrl-C the celerybeat that has acquired the lock. Verify that within 5 seconds the logs emit a statement like New lock acquired by 'scheduler@yyyyyyy' Verify that you see a log statement stating that failover has occurred, and that the other celerybeat is the new primary celerybeat instance Verify that pulp-admin status shows only one scheduler@ entry Verify that no Errors are shown in the logs ### Failover due to killing Start both celerybeat processes as described above. kill -9 the pid of the celerybeat that has the lock. If you start celerybeatA first then it will get the lock. Verify that within 30 seconds the logs emit a statement like New lock acquired by 'scheduler@yyyyyyy' Verify that you see a log statement stating that failover has occurred, and that the other celerybeat is the new primary celerybeat instance Verify that an Error is emitted saying something like: `Worker 'scheduler@xxxx' has gone missing` Verify that pulp-admin status shows only one scheduler@ entry

1. Create an ostree repository and commit a tree using /etc/pulp as the source.
2. Create and sync a repository.
3. List content and note that a content unit was created for the branch HEAD.
4. Make a few more commits:
5. Sync again and list the content.

issue_title: Add support for Pipfiles
POSIX example ignores FRAME_PENDING flag
issue_body:
In POSIX platform example FRAME_PENDING flag is set to true if the local device just sent MAC command frame (before commit b865607619d06c1a10a231025ec48749fef80a19, it checked that it was DATA_REQUEST MAC command).  This creates few issues:

1. The requester (local) device decides whether the responder (remote) device has pending data frame.
2. The requester (local) device ignores responders FRAME_PENDING bit.
3. FRAME_PENDING bit is set to true for all MAC commands (not just DATA_REQUEST).

issue_title: Compiler vrs Compiler issues, vrs IDE issues.
issue_body:
Open thread relies upon a number of standard C header files that are different across compilers, and compiler releases.

For example:

#include <string.h> - depending on the compiler does not have some functions.
This also varies by compiler version, (ie: a fix was introduce in a later version)

In the GNU Autoconf world this is solved via either  "libiberty' or various "#ifdefs"
GCC supports an   #include_next <filename> scheme - other compilers do not.

a) This does not play nicely with IDEs that do not support Autoconf.
b) This also makes code "messy" with lots of #if/#else/#endif fixes.

I propose the following solution:

If a standard header files needs to be fixed for a specific tool.
Then - the following action is taken.

Step 1:

A "pseudo-replacement" header file is created in a new directory called: "fixed"
    #include <openthread/platform/fixed/fixed_FILENAME.H>

Alternatively it could be placed in   ${openthread}/src/<somewhere>/fixed/fixed_FILENAME.H
The concern here is if thread ever provids a "C++" api - with functions defined within a class.

Note: specifically the original filename should not be used "as is", but a standard prefix or suffix be added to the original filename to make it unique. In this example I propose: "fixed_FILENAME.h"

Example:  #include <stdio.h>
Becomes:     #include <openthread/platform/fixed/stdio.h 

Step 2:

This new <openthread/platform/fixed/fixed_FILENAME.H>
Must eventually include the tool chain provided FILENAME.H

But also contains any required compiler/library fixes in the form of:  #if/#else/#endif

Step 3:  Problematic Thread code then includes the "fixed" header file in lue of the standard header file.

issue_title: Fijar un piso de 80% de test coverage y agregar el chequeo al build de Travis
issue_body:
**Contexto**

`pydatajson` es uno de los proyectos de código abierto que como _librería funcional_ consideramos un producto prolijo y acabado.

El código en este repositorio tiene que estar bien documentado, bien testeado y seguir estándares de estilo.

**Propuesta**

Vamos a formalizar el abordaje de estos aspectos de a poco, de manera que incorporemos al build de Travis algunas de estas reglas.

**Segundo:** empezar incorporando un piso de 80% de test coverage para aceptar un build en Travis.

Tener en cuenta que el objetivo de mediano plazo va a ser levantar esta vara al 90%, pero por ahora empezamos por un 80%.

issue_title: support 'kubectl drain'
issue_body:
support equivalent of 'kubectl drain' in pykube.

```
$kubectl drain --help
Drain node in preparation for maintenance. 

The given node will be marked unschedulable to prevent new pods from arriving. 'drain' evicts the pods if the APIServer
supports eviction (http://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/disruptions/). Otherwise, it will use normal DELETE to delete the
pods. The 'drain' evicts or deletes all pods except mirror pods (which cannot be deleted through the API server).  If
there are DaemonSet-managed pods, drain will not proceed without --ignore-daemonsets, and regardless it will not delete
any DaemonSet-managed pods, because those pods would be immediately replaced by the DaemonSet controller, which ignores
unschedulable markings.  If there are any pods that are neither mirror pods nor managed by ReplicationController,
ReplicaSet, DaemonSet, StatefulSet or Job, then drain will not delete any pods unless you use --force.  --force will
also allow deletion to proceed if the managing resource of one or more pods is missing. 

'drain' waits for graceful termination. You should not operate on the machine until the command completes. 

When you are ready to put the node back into service, use kubectl uncordon, which will make the node schedulable again. 

! http://kubernetes.io/images/docs/kubectl_drain.svg

Examples:
  # Drain node "foo", even if there are pods not managed by a ReplicationController, ReplicaSet, Job, DaemonSet or
StatefulSet on it.
  $ kubectl drain foo --force
  
  # As above, but abort if there are pods not managed by a ReplicationController, ReplicaSet, Job, DaemonSet or
StatefulSet, and use a grace period of 15 minutes.
  $ kubectl drain foo --grace-period=900

Options:
      --delete-local-data=false: Continue even if there are pods using emptyDir (local data that will be deleted when
the node is drained).
      --force=false: Continue even if there are pods not managed by a ReplicationController, ReplicaSet, Job, DaemonSet
or StatefulSet.
      --grace-period=-1: Period of time in seconds given to each pod to terminate gracefully. If negative, the default
value specified in the pod will be used.
      --ignore-daemonsets=false: Ignore DaemonSet-managed pods.
      --timeout=0s: The length of time to wait before giving up, zero means infinite

Usage:
  kubectl drain NODE [options]

Use "kubectl options" for a list of global command-line options (applies to all commands).

issue_title: HTTPS redirect loop created
issue_body:
Hello, 
certbot created a redirect loop:
```
cat default_http_to_https_redirect-le-ssl.conf
<IfModule mod_ssl.c>
<VirtualHost *:443>
   ServerAdmin webmaster@localhost
   ServerName mysite.de
   Redirect permanent / https://mysite.de/
SSLCertificateFile /etc/letsencrypt/live/mysite.de/fullchain.pem
SSLCertificateKeyFile /etc/letsencrypt/live/mysite.de/privkey.pem
Include /etc/letsencrypt/options-ssl-apache.conf
</VirtualHost>
</IfModule>
```
I have a :80 virtualhost redirecting to :443 in one config file, and a second config file for :443 with all the website setups. Removing the redirect stopped the loop, but led to another bug: I had two :443 virtualhost config files, my original one and the LE one. Since only the first is read, the site configuration didn't get read. I'll file another bug for that.

issue_title: Bug: YAML serialization of git sha1
issue_body:
In the event that the git short hash of a repository is something like `01234567`, then the image tag is serialized unquoted, resulting in a broken `values.yaml`.

This is a bit hard to reproduce, as it would happen on only ~0.4% of the commits.

You can reproduce the issue by running:
```bash
git clone https://github.com/SwissDataScienceCenter/renku-ui
cd renku-ui
git checkout -b test 0192117
cd helm-chart
chartpress
```

Then, you can check the `values.yaml` file contents:
```bash
cat renku-ui/values.yaml
```
```yaml
[...]
image:
  name: renku/renku-ui
  repository: renku/renku-ui
  tag: 0192117
  pullPolicy: IfNotPresent
[...]
```

As you can see, this will result in `image.tag` equals to `192117` and not `0192117`.

A potential fix would be to always quote sha1 tags.

issue_title: Create plain (no-colour) reporter

We should create two different reporter classes, one which colorizes, and one which doesn't.

issue_title: Readme Encoding Example Error
Assignment Rule Header - disable the default rule?
issue_body:
Thank you all for your hard work, great tool ..
 (this may not be a feature enhancement.. just me thinking too hard.. on a Friday..)

 I'm triggering a condition with my updates that cause the "owner" to change due to the [Default Assignment rule](https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_rest.meta/api_rest/headers_autoassign.htm) in SF. According to the [Restful API documentation](https://resources.docs.salesforce.com/sfdc/pdf/api_rest.pdf) one can pass a header to change this default behavior:

Sforce-Auto-Assign: FALSE

however in api.py:


    def _call_salesforce(self, method, url, **kwargs):
        """Utility method for performing HTTP call to Salesforce.

        Returns a 'requests.result' object.
        """
        headers = {
            'Content-Type': 'application/json',
            'Authorization': 'Bearer ' + self.session_id,
            'X-PrettyPrint': '1'
        }
        result = self.request.request(method, url, headers=headers, **kwargs)


am I missing an obvious way to append to the header or would it be beneficial to add a convention to include additional header items?

issue_title: Smoketest requires solc even if precompiled ABIs are present
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Currently the `smoketest` subcommand unconditionally requires `solc` to be installed even if it might be unnecessary (e.g. in binary distributions that ship with precompiled ABIs)

## Solution

Only require that *either* `solc` or the precompiled ABIs are present (possibly allow forcing one of the two behaviors via cli parameter)

## Tasklist

- [ ] Check that either `solc` or all precompiled ABIs are present
- [ ] (Optional) Add cli parameter to force either `solc` or precompiled check
- [ ] Add a changelog entry.

issue_title: Improve token swap interface
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

The atomic swap has a confusing api:

    def exchange(self, from_asset, from_amount, to_asset, to_amount, target_address):

The arguments in `expect_exchange` are the same as the arguments from `exchange` meaning that the node calling `expect_exchange` has the arguments swapped.

## Solution

Write better documentation.

## Tasklist

- [ ] Doc strings for both functions following (related: issue #465  )

issue_title: Multiple global sections?
issue_body:
There is current a restriction on there can be only one global section and I do not see a compulsive reason why this should be case. I mean, we can simply join multiple global sections.

In practice, this allows us to define a global section whenever we see a need to define a new global function, and do not have to define everything in the only global section in the script.

issue_title: R_library() for github subdir packages
issue_body:
There are cases when R library on github are kept in subdirectories of a repo rather than the root of the repo (e.g. [here](https://github.com/jobovy/extreme-deconvolution/tree/master/r)). Need to adapt `R_library()` for this case. I'd suggest we use a simple syntax:

```
R_library('jobovy/exteme-deconvolution/r')
```
and parse this with SoS to call the `subdir` argument of `devtools::install_github`.

issue_title: Finish Context manager
min_freq=0 bug
issue_body:
**Noticed:**
```
>>>some_field.build_vocab(some_dataset, min_freq=0)
>>>padding_idx = some_field.vocab.stoi['<pad'>]
>>>print(padding_idx, '<pad>')
12 <pad>
```

Looks like <pad> is not equal to 1 which is not okay.

Printed `stoi` and `itos` as well:
```
>>>print(some_field.vocab.stoi)
defaultdict(<function Vocab.__init__.<locals>.<lambda> at 0x103f4f0d0>, {'<pad>': 12, '1': 2, '2': 3, '9': 4, '0': 5, '5': 6, '4': 7, '6': 8, '8': 9, '3': 10, '7': 11, '<unk>': 13})
>>>print(some_field.vocab.itos)
['<unk>', '<pad>', '1', '2', '9', '0', '5', '4', '6', '8', '3', '7', '<pad>', '<unk>']```

**Possible reason:**
Counter subtract does remove the specials but puts their count at 0.
`counter.subtract({tok: counter[tok] for tok in ['<unk>'] + specials})`

**Possible solution:**
Throw an error if `min_freq < 1`

- [ ] Cog

Right now, config will always write when using a context manager, there's no need for this since we should be returning a copy of the data, not the original (see #1855 ). While this can be avoided in some cases by a design choice, there may also be cases where the code doesn't know if something needs to change in the mutable data until checking it. issue_title: Refreshing a live notebook with saved state
issue_body:
I'm not sure how big of an issue this is, but looking at the constructor of [WidgetManager](https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/a9c4069570aa92368f590296569e411d9c49e123/widgetsnbextension/src/manager.js#L55-L129), I notice that:
- If there is saved widget state, `set_state` is called, which checks for live widgets and connects to them etc, and creates any missing widgets.
- Checks if there are any live widgets on the kernel, and if so connects to them and creates the JS comms and widgets for them.

This means that if you have a notebook with widgets, then save the state, and refresh the page without restarting the kernel, the JS comms and widgets are created twice. For certain widget configurations this can lead to an indeterminate state, as duplicates of widgets/comms are created with the same id (only the last one is registered with the manager). At least AFAICT.

issue_title: "Some thoughts on asynchronous API design ..." hushes about asyncio.wait_for()
issue_body:
This is more about "bug report" for https://vorpus.org/blog/some-thoughts-on-asynchronous-api-design-in-a-post-asyncawait-world/ , but for the lack of better place, let me do that here.

So, nowhere the https://vorpus.org/blog/some-thoughts-on-asynchronous-api-design-in-a-post-asyncawait-world/#timeouts-and-cancellation section, or the entire article, mentions asyncio.wait_for() function for timeouts. This may give an impression to a careless reader that asyncio doesn't offer (good) timeout facilities. But well, it does, and it works pretty much as described in that section, except that, well, it requires factoring out the functionality into a separate function instead of allowing intra-function chunks with a context manager. 

(Submitted after seeing this comment https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/issues/149#issuecomment-269604401 , hinting that there may be just a personal preference for context managers ;-).

issue_title: Documentation bug for threadpool and max_workers
issue_body:
Our current tests specify max_workers in the execution block, while the thread pool executor expects
the config option maxThreads. Therefore it defaults to just 2 threads. 

Reported by Michael Wang.

This is to be fixed in documentation and sample configs in tests.

issue_title: [Discussion] Numpy compatibility when possible
issue_body:
This is related to #83 But I want to raise this as a general issue. Some of the current onnx operators are not compatible with numpy.

- min/max are elementwise instead of reduction. In numpy being maximum and minimum
- The broadcast semantics are not ready
- split's output attribute naming.
 
While some of these can be viewed as minor issues. But in general, we can find that the frameworks are leaning toward numpy API. So when we design our in-memory IR in NNVM, we are starting to adopt the principle that: ```use numpy API when such operator exist```

I understand there are concerns about support level and granularity. While these are valid concerns, many of them can actually be addressed by other operators that are not in numpy(e.g. gemm). I am opening this issue to see how other's opinion toward this.

I find the general IR starts into three needs
- coverage
- conciseness
- conventional (reflects conventional usage of API)

While it is easy to design for the first two points. Adopting numpy API when possible hit the third one more easily.

issue_title: Does not use real SPARQL for RDFa test suite
issue_body:
vfaronov, 2010-09-20T12:59:02.000Z 
run_w3c_rdfa_testsuite.py does not use a real SPARQL engine for answering the ASK queries as part of the RDFa test suite. Instead, it always uses an ad-hoc routine for extracting graph patterns. Apparently it wasn't adjusted when SPARQL support was moved to rdfextras.

The following patch adds SPARQL(rdfextras) support back to this test suite, which makes TC #105 and #106 pass, hence they are removed from KNOWN_ISSUES. It leaves the ability to fall back to "_sparql_to_graphs" if real SPARQL doesn't work.
